title: Anaerobic respiration — when oxygen runs out
examTip: AQA regularly asks you to compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration, write word equations, and explain oxygen debt. Make sure you know the different products in animals vs plants/yeast — they are NOT the same.
introTips: This lesson is about what your cells do when they can't get oxygen fast enough — and why it's a trade-off.
You'll learn two different versions of anaerobic respiration: one for animals, one for plants and yeast.
The oxygen debt idea explains why you keep breathing hard even after you stop exercising.
intro: Your body is built around aerobic respiration — it's efficient and clean. But sometimes demand outstrips supply, and your cells need energy faster than oxygen can arrive. That's when anaerobic respiration kicks in. It's a backup system, not a great long-term solution, but it keeps you going when every second counts.

keyPoints.heading: When anaerobic kicks in
keyPoints.content: During intense exercise, your muscles demand energy so rapidly that your lungs and blood can't deliver oxygen fast enough. When cells are short of oxygen, they switch to anaerobic respiration — 'an' means without, so this is respiration without oxygen. The key trade-off is efficiency: aerobic respiration releases a large amount of energy from each glucose molecule; anaerobic releases only a small amount of energy from the same glucose. Your cells are essentially burning through fuel much faster for a lower yield — so it can't be sustained for long.
writeBullets: Anaerobic = respiration WITHOUT oxygen
Triggered when oxygen supply can't meet the muscles' demand — e.g. sprinting
Much less efficient than aerobic — only a small amount of energy released per glucose
Used as a short-term backup; cannot be sustained for long
Both aerobic and anaerobic start with glucose — what happens next differs

keyTerms.def: Respiration that occurs without oxygen — releases a small amount of energy from glucose
Respiration using oxygen — releases a large amount of energy from glucose, producing CO₂ and water
The sugar molecule used as the fuel source in both aerobic and anaerobic respiration

keyPoints.memTip: Anaerobic = AN-oxygen = NO oxygen. The trade-off is always efficiency: aerobic = lots of energy; anaerobic = a little energy. If an exam question asks why anaerobic can't continue for long, the answer is always: it's inefficient AND waste products build up.

keyPoints.heading: Anaerobic respiration in animals
keyPoints.content: In animal cells (including your muscle cells), anaerobic respiration follows this word equation: glucose → lactic acid (+ energy). Notice that no carbon dioxide is produced — this is a common exam trap. The lactic acid doesn't just disappear. It builds up in the muscles, lowering the pH inside the cells. This causes muscle fatigue and the painful sensation of cramps — your muscles literally stop contracting efficiently. The longer the anaerobic effort continues, the more lactic acid accumulates, and the worse the fatigue gets.
writeBullets: Word equation: glucose → lactic acid (+ energy)
No CO₂ produced — unlike aerobic respiration
Lactic acid builds up in muscles → lowers pH
Build-up causes muscle fatigue and cramps
Only a small amount of energy is released — far less than aerobic

keyTerms.def: The waste product of anaerobic respiration in animal cells; builds up in muscles causing fatigue and cramps
The reduction in a muscle's ability to contract, caused by lactic acid build-up during anaerobic respiration
A painful, involuntary muscle contraction associated with lactic acid accumulation

keyPoints.memTip: Animals: glucose → lactic acid. NO CO₂. A good memory hook: L-A-C-T-I-C sounds like 'lacks tick' — it lacks the tick of CO₂ being produced. Repeat the word equation until it's automatic: glucose → lactic acid. That's it.

keyPoints.heading: Anaerobic respiration in plants and yeast
keyPoints.content: Plants and yeast (a fungus) do anaerobic respiration differently. Their word equation is: glucose → ethanol + carbon dioxide (+ energy). This process is called fermentation. Unlike the animal version, CO₂ is produced here. This has two important real-world applications: in brewing beer and wine, yeast ferments sugars in grapes or grains, producing ethanol (the alcohol). In baking bread, yeast ferments sugars in dough and the CO₂ released forms bubbles — making the dough rise and giving bread its light, airy texture. The ethanol evaporates when the bread is baked.
writeBullets: Word equation: glucose → ethanol + carbon dioxide (+ energy)
Called fermentation — carried out by yeast and plants
CO₂ IS produced (unlike animal anaerobic respiration)
Brewing: yeast produces ethanol (alcohol) from sugars
Baking: CO₂ bubbles make dough rise; ethanol evaporates during baking

keyTerms.def: Anaerobic respiration in yeast and plants; glucose is converted to ethanol and carbon dioxide
The alcohol produced by yeast during fermentation of sugars
A single-celled fungus that carries out fermentation, used in brewing and baking

keyPoints.memTip: Yeast/plants: glucose → ethanol + CO₂. Think of a pint of beer: bubbles (CO₂) AND alcohol (ethanol) — both products in one image. The animal version produces lactic acid; the yeast/plant version produces ethanol + CO₂. Getting these mixed up is the classic exam error.

keyPoints.heading: Oxygen debt — paying back the cost
keyPoints.content: After intense exercise, you've noticed that you keep breathing hard even though you've stopped moving. This is because you've built up an oxygen debt — your body needs extra oxygen to deal with the lactic acid that accumulated during anaerobic respiration. The lactic acid is transported in the blood to the liver, where it is oxidised (reacted with oxygen) and converted back to glucose, or broken down further to carbon dioxide and water. Your elevated breathing rate delivers the extra oxygen needed for this. The bigger the oxygen debt, the longer you keep breathing heavily after stopping.
writeBullets: Oxygen debt = extra O₂ needed after exercise to break down lactic acid
Lactic acid is transported in blood to the liver
In the liver, lactic acid is oxidised → CO₂ + water (or converted to glucose)
Breathing rate stays elevated until the debt is paid off
Larger oxygen debt = longer recovery time

keyTerms.def: The amount of extra oxygen needed after exercise to oxidise the lactic acid that built up during anaerobic respiration
To react with oxygen — lactic acid is oxidised in the liver to break it down into CO₂ and water
The period after exercise during which the body returns to its resting state and pays off the oxygen debt

keyPoints.memTip: Oxygen debt is like a financial debt: you borrowed energy without oxygen during the sprint, and now you have to pay it back with interest — by breathing hard until all the lactic acid is dealt with. The liver is the repayment centre. Always include 'liver' and 'oxidised' in any oxygen debt answer.

1.question: Why does anaerobic respiration only release a small amount of energy compared to aerobic respiration?
options: Because anaerobic respiration uses less glucose
Because glucose is only partially broken down when there is no oxygen
Because lactic acid stores the remaining energy permanently
Because anaerobic respiration only happens in the nucleus
1.correctFeedback: Exactly — without oxygen, glucose can't be fully broken down, so most of the energy remains locked in the products (lactic acid or ethanol).
1.wrongFeedback: The key is that without oxygen, glucose can't be completely broken down. Aerobic respiration fully breaks it down to CO₂ and water, releasing all the stored energy. Anaerobic only partially breaks it down, so much of the energy stays locked in the products.

3.question: What are the products of anaerobic respiration in yeast?
3.correctFeedback: Correct — yeast produces ethanol and carbon dioxide. The CO₂ is what makes bread rise and creates bubbles in beer.
3.wrongFeedback: Lactic acid is the product in animal cells, not in yeast. Yeast (and plants) produce ethanol + carbon dioxide — this is fermentation. CO₂ and water are the products of aerobic respiration, not anaerobic.

commonMistakes: Writing that animal anaerobic respiration produces CO₂ — it does NOT. Only the yeast/plant version produces CO₂. Animal anaerobic produces lactic acid only.
Confusing the two anaerobic equations. Animals: glucose → lactic acid. Yeast/plants: glucose → ethanol + CO₂. Learn both word equations separately.
Saying aerobic and anaerobic produce the same amount of energy — anaerobic produces far less. Efficiency is a key difference.
Forgetting that the liver is where lactic acid is broken down during recovery — examiners want you to mention the liver, not just 'the body'.
Saying oxygen debt means you owe oxygen to your muscles — you owe it to the process of breaking down lactic acid in the liver, not the muscles themselves.
examTips: When writing word equations, be precise. Animal: glucose → lactic acid. Yeast: glucose → ethanol + carbon dioxide. Never add CO₂ to the animal equation — it will cost you marks.

For oxygen debt questions, use these three key words: lactic acid, liver, oxidised. A model answer would say: 'Lactic acid is transported to the liver where it is oxidised using the extra oxygen taken in.'

Comparison questions love asking aerobic vs anaerobic — use the table structure in your head: oxygen needed? products? efficiency? Having those three rows ready will structure a full-mark answer quickly.

revisionCardBullets: Anaerobic = WITHOUT oxygen; triggered when O₂ supply can't meet demand during intense exercise
Much less efficient than aerobic — only a small amount of energy released per glucose
Animals: glucose → lactic acid (NO CO₂ produced)
Lactic acid builds up in muscles → lower pH → fatigue and cramps
Yeast/plants: glucose → ethanol + carbon dioxide (this is fermentation)
Fermentation applications: brewing (ethanol), baking (CO₂ makes dough rise)
Oxygen debt = extra O₂ needed after exercise to deal with lactic acid
Lactic acid transported in blood to LIVER where it is oxidised → CO₂ + water
Bigger oxygen debt = more lactic acid = longer recovery breathing
